coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots under Xenon lamp irradiation: Fluorescence red-shift and its potential mechanism
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Hongwei
Tian, Xinmeng
Gong, Yixuan
Ren, Guolan
Liang, Jiangong
coredata.dc:description: Despite extensive research on carbon dots (CDs), rare studies have been performed on the photostability of CDs. Here, the photostability of CDs synthesized with 3-aminobenzoic acid were systematically investigated under different pH conditions (5.0, 7.4 and 9.0). The results showed that under Xenon lamp irradiation, the fluorescence (FL) intensity of the CDs exhibited an increase first and then a decrease, with a gradual shift of the maximum emission wavelength to longer wavelength. Further investigation indicated that the irradiation induced the change of the CD surface functional groups and gave rise to aggregation, resulting in the formation of larger particles. This study provided important reference value towards research on CDs properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photostability
Xenon lamp irradiation
Fluorescence red-shift

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The potential mechanism for FL red-shift of N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of N-CDs (inset: DLS analysis of N-CDs), scale bar: 20nm.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of N-CDs (a) and high-resolution XPS analysis of the C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d).
UV–vis absorption spectrum (blue), fluorescence excitation (black) and emission (red) spectra of the N-CDs. Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs (b) excited with different wavelengths.

Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs (a) and N, B-CDs (b) with the relative fluorescence change of the two CDs irradiated for different periods in pH=7.4PB buffer for a total of 3h (c) and the first 10min (d).
Fluorescence spectra and UV–vis spectra of N-CDs irradiated for different periods in pH5.0 buffer (a, c) and pH9.0 buffer (b, d).
Fluorescence intensity change of the N-CDs irradiated in 180min (a) and the fluorescence intensity change in the first 10min irradiation (b).

(a) HR-TEM image of the N-CDs irradiated for 3h (scale bar: 20nm); the inset shows the magnified structure of the particle in the white circle and the lattice fringe of the graphitic carbon (scale bar: 5nm). (b) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs (black) and N-CDs irradiated for 3h (red).

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots under Xenon lamp irradiation: Fluorescence red-shift and its potential mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, State Key Laboratory of Agricultural Microbiology, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan 430070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Agricultural Microbiology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite extensive research on carbon dots (CDs), rare studies have been performed on the photostability of CDs. Here, the photostability of CDs synthesized with 3-aminobenzoic acid were systematically investigated under different pH conditions (5.0, 7.4 and 9.0). The results showed that under Xenon lamp irradiation, the fluorescence (FL) intensity of the CDs exhibited an increase first and then a decrease, with a gradual shift of the maximum emission wavelength to longer wavelength. Further investigation indicated that the irradiation induced the change of the CD surface functional groups and gave rise to aggregation, resulting in the formation of larger particles. This study provided important reference value towards research on CDs properties.

ce:section.ce:para: During the past decade, carbon dots (CDs) have been attracting broad attention and interest from scientists as a prominent substitute for semiconductors and fluorescent dyes, owing to the distinctive advantages in high fluorescence efficiency [1–4], chemical stability [5,6], satisfactory biocompatibility [7–10] and goodish environmental friendliness [11,12]. To date, considerable methods have been proposed to synthesize CDs, and CDs have shown tremendous potential applications in many fields, such as bioimaging [8,10,13], drug delivery [14–16], antineoplastic medicine [17,18], and biosensors [19,20]. For instance, Jiang et al. synthesized a series of CDs with red-green-blue emission using different phenylenediamines isomers and achieved ensemble emissions by mixing the CDs at a different ratio, enabling the potential application of CDs to two-photon bioimaging in cells and tissues [21]. Feng et al. developed a new tumor treatment strategy by conjugating cisplatin with positively charged CDs to improve the penetration and uptake of the drug, facilitating the accumulation of cisplatin in certain tumor tissues and tumor cell imaging [14]. Wu et al. established a sensitive detection method of organophosphate pesticide through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect of CDs and gold nanoparticles [22]. Many of the aforementioned reports are based on the stable and special optical properties of CDs. However, the photostability of the CDs has not been specifically explored yet.

The photostability plays a very important role in and has a substantial influence on the properties of CDs [23]. While CDs with enhanced stability towards UV and visible light irradiation are feasible to achieve illumination-involved applications [24], their photostability has not been thoroughly studied. Tan et al. reported that after 7h of UV irradiation, CDs produced a longer wavelength FL emission at 500nm [25]. Zhu et al. found that after 5h of UV irradiation, the CDs showed apparent photobleaching [26]. Several other reports only mentioned that CDs were stable under UV irradiation, while the irradiation processes were not investigated in detail [27,28]. The photostability of CDs is usually evaluated by analyzing whether the FL intensity decreases or remains stable after their exposure to UV irradiation, which is related to the emission state of CDs [29–31]. Most of the CDs were excited by visible or UV light, and the influence of full band light irradiation on CDs has not yet been thoroughly studied, suggesting the necessity to explore the influence of full band spectrum irradiation on CDs as well as develop CDs with high photostability.

In this work, nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) with unique fluorescence properties were synthesized through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method, using 3-aminobenzonic acid as the only raw material. The CDs were characterized to possess a uniform size by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Under Xenon lamp irradiation, the emission wavelength and fluorescence (FL) intensity of N-CDs changed with increasing irradiation time. The purpose of this study was to (i) systematically investigate the influence of full band spectrum irradiation on CDs and (ii) explore the potential mechanism for the changes in the optical properties of N-CDs.

The fluorescence measurements were all conducted with a RF–5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The UV–vis absorption measurements were all carried out on a UV–2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were performed on a Nicolet Avatar-330 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA). The zeta-potential and size were measured with a dynamic light scattering (DLS) Malvern Zetasizer instrument (Malvern ZEN 3690) and a JEM–2100F, STEM/EDS high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) (JEOL, Japan). The irradiation experiments were all carried out under a CEL-HXF300 Xe lamp (Wuhan, China). The pH values of the solutions were all measured and adjusted with a PHS-3C pH Meter (Shanghai, China). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were applied to analyze the elemental constituents of the carbon dots with a VG Mutilab 2000 spectrometer (VG Inc., Canada).

3-Aminobenzoic acid, 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid and quinine sulfate were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaOH and CH3COOH, CH3COONa, Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, NaHCO3, Na2CO3 used to prepare buffers were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used directly without further purification and all the solutions were prepared with deionized water obtained from a Millipore water purification system (Milli-Q, Millipore, 18.2MΩ resistivity).

The 3-aminobenzoic acid CDs (N-CDs) used in this study was synthesized using a hydrothermal method modified according to the previous report [32]. In a typical synthesis route, 0.10g 3-aminobenzoic acid was mixed with 10mL deionized water, followed by the addition of saturated NaOH aqueous solution and stirring until full dissolution of 3-aminobenzoic acid. The acquired solution was then transferred into a 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180°C for 4h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the resulting yellow brown supernatant was collected via centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 10min to remove sediment, followed by dialysis against deionized water for 6h to remove unreacted small-molecule impurities. The N-CD solution after dialysis was collected and preserved at 4°C for further use. The concentration of the as-prepared N-CDs was calculated to be 11.27mg/mL by lyophilization in a freeze dryer. The quantum yield of the as-prepared N-CDs was measured and calculated to be 6.5% with a previously reported approach [33].

The N-CD solution synthesized above was 10-fold diluted with 20mmol/L pH7.4 PB buffer. The irradiation experiments were carried out by placing the diluted N-CD solutions under the Xenon lamp and irradiated for different time periods (0s, 20s, 40s, 60s, 80s, 100s, 120s, 150s, 180s, 240s, 300s, 7min, 10min, 15min, 20min, 30min, 40min, 60min, 80min, 110min, 140min, and 180min). Then FL spectra and the UV–vis absorption spectra of the irradiated solutions were recorded.

A similar procedure was carried out in N-CD solutions kept in different pH buffer solutions. The N-CD solutions were maintained at the same concentration after dilution but at a different pH value (5.0 and 9.0) before Xenon lamp irradiation. After irradiation for the same time periods as described above, the FL spectra and the UV–vis absorption spectra of the irradiated N-CD solutions were measured.

The morphology and size distribution of the as-prepared N-CDs were determined by TEM and DLS (Fig. 1 ). The TEM image showed that the N-CDs were well-dispersed with a clear round shape and uniform size before irradiation, which was well supported by the size distribution of the N-CDs in the range of 1.2–2.8nm, with an average of 1.6nm (Fig. 1 inset). The zeta-potential of the N-CDs was detected by the DLS analyzer to be −37.13mV (Fig. S1), indicating N-CDs were rich in carboxyl group and hydroxyl group on the surface. The synthesized N-CDs could remain in a homogeneous phase for several months at 4°C without any apparent precipitation.

The components and surface functional groups of the N-CDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron energy spectrum (XPS) analysis. The full-scan XPS spectrum of N-CDs demonstrated three obvious peaks at the binding energy of 284.60, 399.68, and 531.87eV, which were attributed to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively (Fig. 2a). Besides, the element content analysis based on the corresponding peaks showed that N-CDs contained 72.09% of carbon, 6.60% of nitrogen and 21.31% of oxygen, which was consistent with the peak intensity in Fig. 2a. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s of N-CDs could be resolved into four separate peaks at 283.85, 284.65, 285.60 and 288.40eV, indicating the presence of CC/CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively (Fig. 2b). The N 1s XPS spectrum of N-CDs could be resolved into two peaks, proving the existence of CN at 400.25eV and NH at 399.01eV (Fig. 2c). O 1s XPS spectrum of N-CDs could be resolved into two different signals, CO/NO signal at 533.20eV and CO/NO signal at 531.95eV (Fig. 2d) [31,32,34–36].

The optical properties of the N-CDs were exhaustively investigated by measuring the UV–vis spectra and FL spectra of the N-CDs. In Fig. 3a, the absorption of the as-prepared N-CD solution exhibited an obvious peak at 304nm, which could be attributed to the n-π⁎ transition band of the electrons [37]. The maximum fluorescence excitation peak was recorded at 300nm, while the maximum fluorescence emission peak was located at 405nm. The optical properties of the N-CDs were further characterized by scanning the FL spectrum with a different excitation wavelength. The fluorescent peak of N-CDs remained at 405nm under serial excitation wavelengths from 280 to 350nm (Fig. 3b). No excitation dependent emission was observed, indicating that N-CDs possessed a uniform particle size distribution as reported in a previous study [13].

The photostability of the N-CDs was investigated first by dilution with pH7.4 phosphate buffer before Xenon lamp irradiation. The FL spectra of the solutions were then recorded after irradiation for different periods of time. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CD solution presented an increase to 2.5 times that of the initial value with the prolonging irradiation time in the first 7min of irradiation. From 7 to 180min, the intensity of the N-CDs gradually decreased with the extension of irradiation time, but the intensity showed an overall increase throughout the irradiation process, with the fluorescence of the solution being 1.7 times higher than the original value after 180min of irradiation. In addition, the wavelength of the emission peak lengthened gradually from 464 to 489nm with the extension of time, though the excitation wavelength remained unchanged at 350nm (Fig. 4a).

In order to find out whether all kinds of carbon dots show the same phenomenon of red-shift, we synthesized nitrogen, boron co-doped CDs (N, B-CDs) with a similar synthesis route. The N, B-CDs were thoroughly characterized as shown in Supplementary information. The N, B-CD solution was then diluted with pH7.4 PB buffer and irradiated under Xenon lamp using the same procedure as described for analysis of N-CDs. The UV–vis and FL spectra of N, B-CDs were recorded and the FL intensity was observed to decrease gradually as the irradiation time prolonged. Specifically, the FL intensity of the solution after 180min of irradiation decreased to 0.07 times that of the original solution, while the emission peak of the N, B-CDs remained unchanged at 439nm (Fig. 4b).

Fig. 4c shows the gradual change of the relative FL intensity of the two CDs (N-CDs and N, B-CDs) normalized to the diluted CD solutions. Fig. 4d displays the FL intensity change in the first 10min irradiation.

To further figure out the influence of pH on red-shift of the N-CDs FL spectra, the N-CDs were diluted in 5.0 HAc-NaAc and pH9.0 NaHCO3-Na2CO3 buffer solutions, with the concentration of the N-CDs being kept the same before irradiation. Fig. S4 presents the pH influence on N-CDs fluorescence without Xe lamp irradiation. It can be seen that the FL intensity and peak position remained basically unchanged, which was consistent with our previous report [38]. The results indicated that the N-CD fluorescence was relatively stable in pH5.0, 7.4 and 9.0 buffer solutions. Then the N-CD solutions kept in pH5.0 and 9.0 were irradiated with Xe lamp under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 5a and b, both solutions exhibited an increase of FL intensity after irradiation. In both pH conditions, a slight red-shift of the fluorescence emission peak could be recorded. The emission peak changed from 467 to 489nm in the acid condition and from 467 to 493nm in the alkaline condition. In addition, the absorbance of the N-CDs in both pH buffer solutions was recorded to have shown a gradual increase. Normalized absorbance at 304nm increased from 0.55 to 0.81 in pH5.0 buffer and from 0.60 to 0.80 in pH9.0 buffer (Fig. 5c and d). The darkened color of the solution after irradiation was identical to the higher absorbance.

The irradiation experiments were repeated three times and the changes of the fluorescence intensity are presented in Fig. 6a. As shown in the spectrum, the intensity of the CDs was enhanced, despite a decrease after reaching the maximum intensity as the irradiation time increased. In Fig. 6b, the most rapid intensity enhancement appeared at the first 10min during the irradiation process are shown.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The potential mechanism for FL red-shift of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to clarify the possible mechanism for the red-shift of the FL intensity, HR-TEM and FT-IR were carried out to compare the morphology and surface groups of N-CDs treated or untreated with irradiation for 180min. Fig. 7a shows the HR-TEM image of the N-CDs irradiated for 180min, and obvious aggregation of the N-CDs could be observed. The red-shift of the emission peak of N-CDs may be due to the aggregation of the N-CDs as demonstrated in Fig. 7a, resulting in the formation of larger-sized particles. The CDs of larger size may hold a chance of emitting longer wavelength fluorescence [13].

Liu et al. have reported that under external pressure, the π-π stacking of π-conjugating system enhancement would lead to the red-shift of CDs fluorescence, and the decrease of carbonyl groups resulted in blue-shift of CDs fluorescence [39]. In order to figure out the irradiation influences on the functional groups of N-CDs, FT-IR spectra of N-CDs irradiated or un-irradiated for 180min were measured, and three distinct absorption peaks of the N-CDs could be observed at 3411, 1552 and 1390cm−1, which were attributed to the stretching vibrations of NH and OH at 3411cm−1, CO bonds at 1552cm−1, and CN at 1390cm−1, respectively (Fig. 7b) [30]. In addition, the changes of the surface functional groups on N-CDs were further investigated by measuring the FT-IR spectra of the N-CDs irradiated for 180min. After irradiation, characteristic peaks showed no distinct change in the functional groups of N-CDs, but slight changes were observed in the relative transmittance of the peaks. The stretching vibration of the CO bonds at 1630cm−1 showed a slight increase while the CN bonds were regarded as unchanged, indicating the potential oxidation of some functional groups into carbonyl groups during the irradiation. Liu et al. have reported that longer wavelength fluorescence could be attributed to the defects states with oxygenous groups [40]. Thus, the red-shift of N-CDs may be also related to the oxidation of functional groups. The aforementioned results indicate that the red-shift of the fluorescence emission peaks might arise from both the aggregation of the CDs and the change of the surface functional groups.

We have successfully synthesized N-CDs with promising fluorescence properties. The CDs prepared with 3-aminobenzic acid presented a brand-new red-shift property after Xenon lamp irradiation. The fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs varied along with the irradiation time, with an increase in the first few minutes followed by a decrease from the maximum intensity, and ending up with an integrated increase of the fluorescence intensity after irradiation. The integrated change of fluorescence intensity increased after irradiation. The maximum fluorescence wavelength showed gradual red-shift to longer wavelength during the irradiation. The mechanism of the red-shift may be related to the aggregation of the CDs and the change of surface functional groups during the irradiation. The aggregation leads to the formation of larger size carbon nanoparticles, and thus the emission of longer wavelength fluorescence. The detailed mechanism of the red-shift calls for further investigation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31772785), National Key Research and Development Program (2016YFD0500105), and Da Bei Nong Group Promoted Project for Young Scholar of HZAU (2017DBN009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis methods, UV–vis spectrum, FL spectra, XPS characterization and quantum yield measurements of N, B-CDs. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.03.020.
